lines.original: Mai keha yaariyan ch jatta teri meri da ni hunda
lines.translated: I say in friendship, bro, there's no 'yours and mine'.
lines.note: 'jatta' = vocative 'bro' (see glossary). 'ni hunda' = 'doesn't happen' — implying possessions/claims don't exist within true friendship.

lines.original: Jadaan pakki aale aa saanu neri da ni hunda
lines.translated: When we're the true/firm ones, jealousy doesn't happen to us.
lines.note: 'neri' is ambiguous in the original; I rendered it conservatively as 'jealousy'. 'pakki aale' = 'true/loyal ones.'

lines.original: Sutta peya sher kade chhedi da ni hunda
lines.translated: With a cigarette in his mouth, a lion's never one to be provoked.
lines.note: 'Sutta peya' refers to smoking/a lit cigarette; 'sher' is a metaphor for a tough/proud man—line implies someone too tough to be messed with.

lines.original: Kalla dekh ke bagana putt gheri da ni hunda
lines.translated: Just because you see me alone and run off, son — an ambush won't work.
lines.note: I read 'bagana' as 'bhagana' (to run away). 'Putt' is a vocative 'son/kid'; 'gheri' means 'ambush/encirclement.' Translation kept literal and conservative.

lines.original: 5-7 cheejan hun bikaneri pehli hove
lines.translated: Now, out of 5–7 things, Bikaneri should come first.
lines.note: “Bikaneri” literally means someone or something from Bikaner (a city in Rajasthan). Here it's ambiguous—could refer to a person, a style, or a thing associated with Bikaner.

lines.original: Dooji hove bande khaane tor jivein baili hove
lines.translated: Second, men eat like buffaloes.
lines.note: baili = buffalo; comparing someone's eating to a buffalo implies heavy, messy, or greedy eating in Punjabi imagery.

lines.original: Teeja chale naam pura gal saare faili hove
lines.translated: When the third one moves, his full name and all the talk spread.

lines.original: Aava pindo aiddan lagge biba jivein rally hove
lines.translated: She comes to the village like there's a rally, girl
lines.note: 'biba' = girl (affectionate); 'pindo' = village. Line suggests she arrives so flashy the village looks like a rally.

lines.original: Kade time si kehndi si ki aa palle palle jatta
lines.translated: There were times she used to say, "Come close, come close, bro."
lines.note: 'jatta' is a vocative from 'jatt' used as 'bro'/'young man'; 'aa palle palle' here means 'come close' or 'come to my side.'

lines.original: Kehndi dekh ke ferrari kitthe challe kalle jatta
lines.translated: She says, looking at me, 'Where will the Ferrari go alone, jatta?'
lines.note: ‘jatta’ is a vocative (jatt / bro) per glossary; 'kalle' means 'alone'. Translation kept literal.

lines.original: Jinne opp ne saare ve terе thalle thalle jatta
lines.translated: All the opps are under you, jatt
lines.note: ‘opp(s)’ = opponents/rivals; 'thalle thalle' (repetition) emphasizes 'beneath/under your feet'. 'jatta' is a vocative (see glossary: jatt / bro).

lines.original: Naddi dekh kе chadhayi kehndi balle balle jatta
lines.translated: Seeing the river, the swell rises and says, "balle balle, jatta."
lines.transliteration: Naddi dekh ke chadhayi kehndi balle balle jatta
lines.note: ‘balle balle’ is a celebratory exclamation (left untranslated here); ‘jatta’ is a vocative address meaning ‘jatt / bro’ (per glossary). ‘chadhayi’ is ambiguous—rendered conservatively as ‘swell/rise’ rather than a poetic metaphor.

lines.original: 5-7 cheeja hon pehli kaali ghodi hove
lines.translated: There should be five to seven things; the first should be a black mare.

lines.original: Dooji beeba farm te cheeneyan di jodi hove
lines.translated: Second, a girl on the farm and a pair of Chinese.
lines.note: 'beeba' = 'girl / dear girl' (from glossary). The word 'cheeneyan' is ambiguous here — literally 'Chinese' (e.g., Chinese people/women) but could be a misheard word (for example, 'chains' or another local term); context doesn't fully disambiguate.

lines.original: Teeji hove anakh ve maaya chahe thodi hove
lines.translated: Let pride be third, even if wealth is little.
lines.note: anakh = pride/self-respect; maaya = wealth/material things. 'Teeji' = 'third' (placing pride third among priorities); 've' is a vocative/emphatic particle.

lines.original: Chadh ke aayi nu bhajj dooni ghar modi hove
lines.translated: If she comes riding, both will run and the house will be turned.
lines.note: The line is dialectal and ambiguous. 'Bhajj dooni' could mean 'both will run' or 'she'll run twice'; 'ghar modi hove' literally 'the house will be turned' — possibly 'the household will be upset/changed' depending on regional usage. Original is already romanized, so transliteration omitted.

lines.original: Saade jigre nu dekhi beeba matta nu na dekhi
lines.translated: Look at our guts; don't look at the girl's face.
lines.note: 'jigre' = guts/courage; 'beeba' = girl (see glossary). 'matta' is ambiguous here—rendered conservatively as 'face'.

lines.original: Takkaan nu naa dekhi ehna satta nu na dekhi
lines.translated: Don't look at the weak, don't look at their power.
lines.note: The word 'takkaan' is dialectal/ambiguous — here taken conservatively as 'the weak/lowly'. 'Satta' means 'power' or 'authority.' Translation is kept literal rather than poetic.

lines.original: Jor gaaddaran chon hunda beeba chatta nu na dekhi
lines.translated: Strength comes from traitors — beeba, don't look at the betrayer.
lines.note: beeba = 'girl / dear girl' (glossary). 'gaaddaran' means 'traitors.' The word 'chatta' is ambiguous in this line; I rendered it conservatively as 'betrayer' to match 'gaaddaran.'

lines.original: Sirr ghum jau rakaane ehna akhan nu na dekhi
lines.translated: Keep your head turned—don't look into these eyes.

lines.original: Panj saat cheejan hun pehli yaari pakki hove
lines.translated: Five or seven things now — first, let friendship be firm.
lines.note: “Panj saat” is colloquial for 'a few' or 'several.' “Yaari” means friendship/loyalty (see glossary). “Pakki hove” = 'be firm/secure/confirmed.'

lines.original: Dooja paaya kurta te jeep thalle rakhi hove
lines.translated: Secondly, he'd have kept a kurta under the jeep.
lines.note: ‘Dooja paaya’ = 'secondly'. Literal image: 'kept a kurta under the jeep.' Likely a boastful detail about possessions (owning a jeep) rather than a standard idiom.

lines.original: Teeji hovi ohth ohdi ehna munda lucky hove
lines.translated: May her third one be there — may those boys be lucky.
lines.note: 'teeji' literally means 'third' (ambiguous here — could mean a third person or item). 'munda' = boys.

lines.original: Latta khich khich jaanda jor laala thakki hove
lines.translated: He keeps tugging and tugging; with all his strength the lad becomes tired.
lines.note: “Latta khich khich” literally means repeatedly tugging/pulling. “Laala” is a colloquial male address (like 'lad' or 'guy'); “jor” means force/strength.

lines.original: Fer ki karegi mile nitt ni je
lines.translated: Then what will she do if she doesn't meet him every day?
lines.note: ‘karegi’ marks a female subject. ‘je’ = if; ‘nitt’ = daily. The original doesn't specify who is being 'met'—rendered here as 'him' as a natural interpretation common in songs, but the object is ambiguous.

lines.original: Dass main ki kara ni laga chitt ni je
lines.translated: Tell me what I should do if my heart isn't in it.
lines.note: 'chitt' here means mind/heart; 'ni je' is a conditional 'if'—so this is a literal, conservative reading: 'if my heart isn't in it.'

lines.original: Mere sohniye dream kuch hor aithe
lines.translated: My beautiful one, dreams are different here

lines.original: Jor baahaan ch chahida jang jitt ni je
lines.translated: You need strong arms — you can't win the war if you don't.
lines.note: ‘Jor baahaan’ literally means 'strong arms' (physical strength or supportive backing). ‘Jang’ is 'war' or 'fight' and ‘je’ means 'if' — the line says that without strength/support the battle can't be won.

lines.original: Paanj saat cheejan pehla lv da leeda hove
lines.translated: Five or seven things — the first should be an LV item.
lines.note: 'lv' is ambiguous in the original; it likely refers to LV (Louis Vuitton) or could be shorthand for 'love'. Context doesn't make the meaning clear, so 'LV' is kept literal.

lines.original: Dooja kuch karne da andar koi keeda hove
lines.translated: Second, there should be some bug inside to do something
lines.note: ‘keeda’ literally means 'insect' but here idiomatically refers to an inner itch/obsession or restless urge to act.

lines.original: Teeja hove yaari aisa jebo chahe peeda hove
lines.translated: Let the third be a friendship such that even if there is pain.
lines.note: yaari = friendship/loyalty (see glossary). The word "jebo" in the line is unclear/ambiguous; translation treats the phrase as 'aisa... chahe peeda hove' = 'such that even if there is pain.'

lines.original: Jithe akhaan adh jaave sachi ehna cheeda hove
lines.translated: Where the eyes half-close, truly such teasing exists.
lines.note: 'adh jaave' means eyes half-close; 'cheeda' (from 'chedna') refers to teasing/flirtiness; 'sachi' is an emphatic 'truly/indeed.'

lines.original: Mere kurta hove te tera booti aala suit
lines.translated: If I'm wearing a kurta and you're wearing a booti-style suit
lines.note: ‘Booti’ refers to a small floral/embroidered motif on clothing; here it describes the style of the suit.

lines.original: Chete kari firre tu ghurale aala route
lines.translated: You keep remembering, you wander that winding route
lines.note: ‘ghurale’ is ambiguous here; interpreted conservatively as 'curvy/winding'. 'Chete kari firre tu' = 'you keep remembering/you keep thinking (about it).'

lines.original: Main keha mere naal chali jado jaave gedi route
lines.translated: I said, come with me when I go for a joyride.
lines.note: ‘gedi route’ = cruising around/going for a joyride; colloquial phrase for driving around town for fun.

lines.original: Dekhi dedh minute bich vajju dedh sau salute mai keha
lines.translated: I said to fire one hundred fifty salutes within one and a half minutes.
lines.note: dedh minute = one and a half minutes; dedh sau = 150; 'salute' refers to gun salutes; 'vajju' is colloquial here, interpreted as 'to fire/play' (ambiguous in dialect).

lines.original: 5-7 cheejan hun pehli bebe khush hove
lines.translated: Five or seven things now — first, may the dear girl be happy.
lines.note: “bebe” is a variant of biba/beeba (girl/dear girl). “5-7” is a loose count meaning a few items; “khush hove” is a wish (‘may be happy’).

lines.original: Dooji meri baapu di haye rabb valla mucch hove
lines.translated: Second, may my father's mustache be blessed by God.
lines.note: In Punjabi culture a mustache often stands for pride/honor; 'haye rabb valla' is an exclamatory invocation meaning roughly 'oh God' or 'God-blessed.'

lines.original: Teeja saala area ch naam hove puchh hove
lines.translated: Third, may my name be known in the area, may people ask about me.
lines.note: 'Teeja' = 'third' (enumerating). 'saala' here is a colloquial intensifier/expletive common in Punjabi (not meant literally). 'puchh hove' implies people ask about or inquire after him.

lines.original: Meri jaan jaavein mere yaaran nu na kuch hove
lines.translated: Even if I lose my life, may nothing happen to my friends.
lines.note: 'Meri jaan jaavein' = 'even if I lose my life / die.' 'yaaran' = friends (implies close loyalty — see glossary entry 'yaari').

lines.original: Ni main gaddiyan na thaalda barola jitho langga
lines.translated: I don't park cars where Barola passes
lines.note: Ni is a vocative particle. 'Barola' appears to be a name or place; 'thaalda' is taken conservatively as 'park/stop' (literal meaning uncertain).

lines.original: Ni main maadeyaan li maada biba changeyaan layi changga
lines.translated: Hey, I'm bad for the bad girls, and good for the good ones.
lines.note: biba = girl (see glossary). The phrase 'maadeyaan li' is ambiguous; here read literally as 'for the bad ones,' yielding a conservative 'bad-for-bad, good-for-good' meaning.

lines.original: Jaane neeli chhat aala dass ohto kahda sanga
lines.translated: Tell me which companion is from that blue-roofed place.
lines.note: Ambiguous: 'Jaane' can be a vocative 'jaan(e)' (beloved) or part of an imperative/request; I translated conservatively as 'Tell me'. 'neeli chhat aala' is rendered literally as 'blue-roofed place/house.'

lines.original: Bas paanj saat cheejan ohto jyada naa mai manga mai keha
lines.translated: I said I'd only ask for five or seven things, not more than that.
lines.note: “paanj saat” literally means ‘five or seven’ used colloquially for ‘a few’; “ohto” here means ‘than that’ / ‘from that’ (i.e., not more than that).

lines.original: Panj saat cheejan pehla daang utte dera hove
lines.translated: Let five or seven things be camped on the front line.
lines.note: Literal rendering: 'panj saat' = 'five or seven' (approx.); 'dera' = 'camp/settlement'. The word 'daang' is ambiguous here—rendered conservatively as 'front line/post.'

lines.original: Dooji akh baaj aali sher jeha jera hove
lines.translated: The other eye would be hawk-like, the kind a lion would have
lines.note: Uses animal metaphors: 'baaj' (hawk) implies sharp eyesight/alertness, 'sher' (lion) implies strength or courage.

lines.original: Teeja aida vedha hove kile jeda ghera hove
lines.translated: The third should be so strange, like a fort that's surrounded.
lines.transliteration: ਤੀਜਾ ਐਦਾ ਵੇਧਾ ਹੋਵੇ ਕਿਲੇ ਜੇਦਾ ਘੇਰਾ ਹੋਵੇ

lines.original: 5 7 cheejan hun hor na bathera hove
lines.translated: There shouldn't be many other things now besides five and seven.
lines.note: '5 7' references the song/title; 'bathera' means 'many' or 'a lot'.
